perturbation.original_text: Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to consider encouraging future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to perhaps procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom, if convenient, in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers.
perturbation.explanation: The changes introduce ambiguity by replacing 'commercially reasonable efforts' with 'consider encouraging,' 'procure' with 'perhaps procure,' and adding 'if convenient,' making the obligation of the seller to encourage customers to use CompuCom's products vague and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the encouragement to use CompuCom's products so vague and non-committal, it creates an ambiguous obligation. If the encouragement is interpreted as a weak recommendation, and if CompuCom and Seller together hold significant market power, it can be argued that this agreement is an unreasonable restraint of trade violating Section 1 of the Sherman Act, as it could discourage customers from seeking other potentially better or more competitive options for Products and image load/configuration.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. (July 2, 1890, ch. 647, § 1, 26 Stat. 209 ; Aug. 17, 1937, ch. 690, title VIII, 50 Stat. 693 ; July 7, 1955, ch. 281, 69 Stat. 282 ; Pub. L. 93–528, § 3 , Dec. 21, 1974 , 88 Stat. 1708 ; Pub. L. 94–145, § 2 , Dec. 12, 1975 , 89 Stat. 801 ; Pub. L. 101–588, § 4(a) , Nov. 16, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2880 ; Pub. L. 108–237, title II, § 215(a) , June 22, 2004 , 118 Stat. 668 .) L. 101–588 substituted “$10,000,000” for “one million dollars” and “$350,000” for “one hundred thousand dollars”. 1975— Pub. L. 93–528 substituted “a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding one million dollars if a corporation, or, if any other person, one hundred thousand dollars, or by imprisonment not exceeding three years” for “a misdemeanor, and on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding f
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces firm obligations with vague suggestions. The original text uses 'commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers...to procure Products...from CompuCom', while the changed text says Seller will 'consider encouraging future Service customers...to perhaps procure Products...from CompuCom, if convenient'. This introduces significant ambiguity and weakens the obligation. The Law Explanation states that such vagueness could be an unreasonable restraint of trade violating Section 1 of the Sherman Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Every contract, combination...in restraint of trade or commerce...is declared to be illegal'. By weakening the obligation to encourage procurement from CompuCom, the modified contract introduces ambiguity that, if CompuCom and the seller have significant market power, could be interpreted as discouraging customers from seeking competitive options, thereby restraining trade. The original text was a clear instruction, and now is very ambiguous, which is why this is a legal contradiction. The perturbation definition indicates that an ambiguous legal obligation that makes it difficult to enforce under state or national law can result in non-compliance and legal obligations open to dispute, which is the case here.

perturbation.original_text: CompuCom agrees to notify Seller of CompuCom's rejection of such orders for Product within a mutually agreed upon time that is reasonable under the time constraints required by the customer order or contract.
perturbation.changed_text: CompuCom should endeavor to inform Seller of CompuCom's potential rejection of such orders for Product within a time that may or may not be seen as reasonable, depending on circumstances surrounding the customer order or contract.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'agrees to notify' with 'should endeavor to inform,' 'rejection' with 'potential rejection,' 'mutually agreed upon time that is reasonable' with 'a time that may or may not be seen as reasonable', making the notification obligation highly subjective and unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Under UCC § 2-206, an offer to make a contract shall be construed as inviting acceptance in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances. This modification, by making the time frame for rejection notification so vague ('may or may not be seen as reasonable'), introduces uncertainty about when an offer is deemed accepted or rejected, potentially conflicting with the UCC's requirement for clarity in offer and acceptance in commercial transactions and good faith dealing.  The seller is left in a legal gray area, as they may have changed operations to accommodate the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract. (1) Unless otherwise unambiguously indicated by the language or circumstances (a) an offer to make a contract shall be construed as inviting acceptance in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances; (b) an order or other offer to buy goods for prompt or current shipment shall be construed as inviting acceptance either by a prompt promise to ship or by the prompt or current shipment of conforming or non-conforming goods, but such a shipment of non-conforming goods does not constitute an acceptance if the seller seasonably notifies the buyer that the shipment is offered only as an accommodation to the buyer. (2) Where the beginning of a requested performance is a reasonable mode of acceptance an offeror who is not notified of acceptance within a reasonable time may treat the offer as having lapsed before acceptance. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity that undermines the clarity required for contract formation under UCC § 2-206. The original text specifies a 'mutually agreed upon time that is reasonable,' while the changed text uses 'a time that may or may not be seen as reasonable.' This directly contradicts the UCC's intent, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'an offer to make a contract shall be construed as inviting acceptance in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances.' The modification makes the terms of acceptance and rejection uncertain, leading to a legal contradiction and potentially conflicting with the UCC.

perturbation.original_text: Seller shall not, directly or indirectly, use, sell, license, publish, disclose, or otherwise transfer or make available to others any of CompuCom's Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller should avoid using, selling, licensing, publishing, disclosing, or otherwise transferring or making available to others any of CompuCom's Confidential Information, if reasonably possible.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not' with 'should avoid' and adding 'if reasonably possible' introduces a significant degree of ambiguity. The obligation to protect confidential information becomes discretionary and less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This weakened obligation contradicts the spirit and intent of the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA), which provides legal remedies against the misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the protection of CompuCom's confidential information discretionary ('if reasonably possible'), the modified text undermines the DUTSA's aim of ensuring that trade secrets are protected from unauthorized use or disclosure.  If a secret is disclosed 'if reasonably possible,' the seller has not misappropriated any trade secrets.

Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that the Seller "shall not" disclose confidential information. The changed text weakens this obligation to "should avoid...if reasonably possible." This introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation discretionary. According to the Law Explanation, this undermines the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA)'s aim of protecting trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "Misappropriation" as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By adding 'if reasonably possible', the disclosure of trade secrets may not be considered misappropriation, and therefore, there is no breach of contract or duty.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests, demands or other communications required by or otherwise with respect to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given to any party when delivered personally (by courier service or otherwise), when delivered by facsimile and confirmed by return facsimile, or five business days after being mailed by first-class mail, postage prepaid in each case to the applicable addresses set forth below:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests, demands or other communications related to this Agreement may be delivered in a manner that seems appropriate at the time. If a party alleges that a notice was duly given, it will be considered on a case-by-case basis.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the specific requirements for notice (writing, delivery methods, confirmation) and replaces them with a vague standard of 'a manner that seems appropriate at the time' and 'considered on a case-by-case basis.' This introduces significant uncertainty and subjectivity into the notice provisions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Contract Law - Principles of Contract Interpretation - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware law, implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute per se, Delaware contract law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By eliminating the specific notice requirements and replacing them with a subjective and uncertain standard, the modification creates the potential for parties to act in bad faith by claiming lack of proper notice. This undermines the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law, as it removes objective standards for determining whether notice was properly given, leading to potential disputes and unfair practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity in the notice requirements, potentially contradicting the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original contract provides specific, objective standards for giving notice, while the modified version relies on subjective interpretation ('a manner that seems appropriate at the time' and 'considered on a case-by-case basis'). This subjectivity undermines the predictability and reliability of notice provisions, creating an environment where parties could act in bad faith by claiming lack of proper notice. The scraped snippet defines "good faith" as encompassing "honest dealing," "faithful performance of duties," and "observance of fair dealing standards." By replacing the objective notice requirements with a subjective standard, the modified contract creates the potential for parties to act in bad faith, which undermines the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing and increases the risk of disputes and unfair practices.
